EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakly sheared, high instability day in August resulted in isolated storms that were able to produce damaging winds and an isolated tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts resulted in several trees being downed along David Bruce Ave. in Charlotte Courthouse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front over the region combined with ample instability and marginal shear allowed for the development of isolated, pulse severe storms across the Mid-Atlantic into the early hours of August 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds from a severe thunderstorm snapped numerous large limbs that fell in Horseshoe Flats Campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front over the region combined with ample instability and marginal shear allowed for the development of isolated, pulse severe storms across the Mid-Atlantic into the early hours of August 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm with damaging winds blew three trees down along Pocahontas Trail Road. One of the trees brought down powerlines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front over the region combined with ample instability and marginal shear allowed for the development of isolated, pulse severe storms across the Mid-Atlantic into the early hours of August 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe storms with damaging wind gusts knocked several trees down along Clarkton Road, in the Nathalie area. Some of these trees brought down power lines with them, resulting in numerous power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture, ushered into our area by a southwesterly low level jet, overran easterly wind flow associated with high pressure wedging near the surface. Precipitable water values per the RNK/GSO 31/12Z soundings were 1.64���/2.04��� respectively.  Despite abundant moisture, instability remained strictly limited, preventing high rainfall rates from occurring.  As such, widespread 1 to 3 inch rainfall amounts were observed, but most of which fell over a 4 to 8 hour period of time under rainfall rates averaging 0.1 to 0.3 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cooperative Weather Observer (COOP) at Stuart, VA recorded 2.06 inches of rainfall for the 24-hour period ending 7 am on September 1st.  Most of this rainfall fell during the morning and afternoon of August 31st.  This rainfall amount sets a new daily record for the Stuart COOP for September 1st, breaking the previous record of 2.05 inches set in 2007.  Records for this station date back to May 1913.

This rainfall amount sets a new daily record for the Meadows-of-Dan COOP for September 1st, breaking the previous record of 1.96 inches set in 1959. Records for this station date back to December 1950.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Patrick County 911 Center reported that Commerce Street in the Town of Stuart is closed off due to Campbell Branch overflowing its banks several inches deep across the roadway.  A personal mesonet station in Stuart recorded roughly 1.8 inches of rainfall between 1130 am and 1230 pm EDT, between a 2- and a 5-year rainfall event per NOAA Atlas-14 point frequency estimates, though soils were becoming saturated from rainfall earlier in the day before this convective band moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the most significant flooding events in years occurred during November 11th and 12th, given both the amounts of rainfall and the footprint where flooding occurred.  In the wake of high pressure passing east off the mid-Atlantic coast, a plume of deep tropical moisture was rapidly carried inland to the central Appalachians ahead of a slow-moving cold front.  Though this plume was of tropical origin, it was not directly related to Tropical Storm Eta, which was off the coast of southern Florida.  Precipitable Water values were in the extremely high range of 1.9 to 2.2 inches with this airmass, around the 99th percentile for mid-November.  In addition, evapotranspiration was significantly lower than during the previous few months given lower daytime heating and the fact that most vegetation had become dormant for the upcoming winter months.  Despite dry conditions for almost two weeks leading up to the event, numerous instances of flooding and flash flooding occurred as 4 to 6 inches of rain fell across portions of the Piedmont on the 11th, followed by an additional 1 to 3 inches on the 12th.  Some of the activity was convective in nature, producing locally 2 to 4 inch per hour rainfall rates.  The rainfall produces numerous instances of flooding and flash flooding, followed shortly by river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trade Street was reported closed due to flooding from the Dan River when the river was above a stage of about 23.5 feet beginning around 3:00 pm on November 12th.  The river did not recede below this stage until late afternoon on the 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias moved up the east coast, passing through southern Maryland on the morning of Tuesday, August 4th, 2020, spawning several tornadoes as well as flooding rain and tropical storm force winds. ||Storm total rainfall ranged from 2 to 4 inches near Interstate 95 to as much as 9 inches in Calvert County east of Interstate 95. The heavy rain led to numerous incidents of flooding and flash flooding, especially near and east of Interstate 95. ||The highest sustained wind of 46 knots was reported at Bishops Head and the highest peak gust of 63 knots was also reported at Bishops Head. ||There were three tornadoes associated with Isaias over southern Maryland and there were also numerous reports of downed trees from tropical storm force winds, especially near and east of Interstate 95. ||A storm surge of 1 to 2 feet occurred along the western shore of the Chesapeake Bay and the shore of the Tidal Potomac River. This did result in minor tidal Flooding in Washington DC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The King George County 911 Center received nearly three dozen calls for trees, wires, and branches down. A tree also fell onto a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Isaias tracked north just inland of the Middle Atlantic Coast late Monday night, August 3rd through Tuesday morning, August 4th. The Tropical Storm produced heavy rain which caused minor flooding across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totals between three and five inches were reported across the county. Rainfall total of 4.57 inches was reported at (11 SW) Emporia. Rainfall total of 4.21 inches was reported at Purdy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front over the region combined with ample instability and marginal shear allowed for the development of isolated, pulse severe storms across the Mid-Atlantic into the early hours of August 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe storm producing damaging wind gusts knocked a tree down onto German Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front over the region combined with ample instability and marginal shear allowed for the development of isolated, pulse severe storms across the Mid-Atlantic into the early hours of August 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe storm producing damaging winds knocked several trees down onto Old Concord Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front, draped across the foothills and Piedmont and supported aloft by multiple waves of energy entering from the Tennessee Valley, provided the triggers for  scattered slow-moving thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon across the mountains and foothills.  Strong daytime heating provided the instability to allow some storms to intensify to severe levels, producing damaging winds and hail.  In addition, deep tropical moisture in place in the wake of Tropical Storm Isaias allowed some storms to produce heavy rain, with 2-4 inch per hour rainfall rates at times.  Light winds aloft made for slow storm movement, allowing storms to remain over the same locations for extended periods of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe storm produced damaging winds, which knocked a tree onto a powerline along Mountain Valley Road, near the county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain occurred as tropical moisture (record levels for November) streamed northward into the area and interacted with a stalled frontal zone. This heavy rainfall resulted in widespread flooding across Northern and Central Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rappahannock River at Fredericksburg was in Minor Flood stage during the indicated time period, reaching a peak level of 16.40 feet at 8:00 PM on November 12th. Water reached River Road on the east side of the river. Old Mill Park could no longer be accessed and was closed due to flooding. Water entered a basement near Fredericksburg city dock, and water entered a guest house off Caroline Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe storms producing damaging winds brought a tree down that blocked Plantation Drive, near Axton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on Middle River at Grottoes, VA exceeded the 12 foot flood stage during the indicated period. Fields along the river began to flood at this stage. The peak level of 12.47 feet occurred at 5:30 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture was observed lifting across the mid-Atlantic region ahead of a deep negatively-tilted trough passing east across the Ohio River Valley.  Deep moisture convergence was observed across the Piedmont where a southerly low-level jet across the Carolinas brought unseasonably-moist air across the Piedmont region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 4 inches of rain fell during the late evening hours of the 29th into early morning of the 30th.  This resulted in localized minor stream flooding, including along Buffalo Creek, which was observed flowing across Homewood Drive about 1 to 3 inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Isaias tracked north just inland of the Middle Atlantic Coast from late Monday night, August 3rd through Tuesday morning, August 4th. Outer bands north and east of the Tropical Storm produced seven tornadoes across portions of eastern and southeast Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the NWS Wakefield office found a path of storm damage consistent with an EF1 tornado. The damage began near the Great Dismal Swamp National Wildlife Refuge near the Virginia/North Carolina border, and ended just south of Everets Road in the northern City of Suffolk. The tornado caused significant damage to at least 8 buildings in downtown Suffolk. The tornado then moved north and crossed Lake Mead where it uprooted or snapped numerous trees before lifting, as it approached the Isle of Wight County line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cooperative Weather Observer for Appomattox, VA (APXV2) recorded a storm-total rainfall amount of 9.20 inches rainfall during a 72-hour period ending 12Z on November 13th.  This is the highest 3-day total ever for November, and the 4th- highest ever for any month.  During the 24-hour period ending 12Z on the 12th, 7.28 inches fell, the highest 24-hour precipitation ever recorded during November, and 2nd-highest of any month for this station.  Both the 7.28 inch measurement for a 24-hour period, and 9.20 inch storm-total measurement over 72 hours are each around 50-year ARI events per NOAA Atlas 14 point frequency estimates. Records for this station date back to November 1937.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the most significant flooding events in years occurred during November 11th and 12th, given both the amounts of rainfall and the footprint where flooding occurred.  In the wake of high pressure passing east off the mid-Atlantic coast, a plume of deep tropical moisture was rapidly carried inland to the central Appalachians ahead of a slow-moving cold front.  Though this plume was of tropical origin, it was not directly related to Tropical Storm Eta, which was off the coast of southern Florida.  Precipitable Water values were in the extremely high range of 1.9 to 2.2 inches with this airmass, around the 99th percentile for mid-November.  In addition, evapotranspiration was significantly lower than during the previous few months given lower daytime heating and the fact that most vegetation had become dormant for the upcoming winter months.  Despite dry conditions for almost two weeks leading up to the event, numerous instances of flooding and flash flooding occurred as 4 to 6 inches of rain fell across portions of the Piedmont on the 11th, followed by an additional 1 to 3 inches on the 12th.  Some of the activity was convective in nature, producing locally 2 to 4 inch per hour rainfall rates.  The rainfall produces numerous instances of flooding and flash flooding, followed shortly by river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported a storm-total rainfall amount of over 10 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front, draped across the foothills and Piedmont and supported aloft by multiple waves of energy entering from the Tennessee Valley, provided the triggers for  scattered slow-moving thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon across the mountains and foothills.  Strong daytime heating provided the instability to allow some storms to intensify to severe levels, producing damaging winds and hail.  In addition, deep tropical moisture in place in the wake of Tropical Storm Isaias allowed some storms to produce heavy rain, with 2-4 inch per hour rainfall rates at times.  Light winds aloft made for slow storm movement, allowing storms to remain over the same locations for extended periods of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe storm produced damaging winds, knocking a tree down on Wolf Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving front across the Mid-Atlantic interacted with a very unstable airmass that was currently in place to produce a severe storm in the Lynchburg area with damaging straight-line winds, resulting in numerous reports of property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe storm generating damaging winds caused numerous trees, limbs, as well as some powerlines, to fall along a path in the Forest area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving front across the Mid-Atlantic interacted with a very unstable airmass that was currently in place to produce a severe storm in the Lynchburg area with damaging straight-line winds, resulting in numerous reports of property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe storm produced a swath of wind damage across the southern part of the City of Lynchburg, from Laxton Road to Candlers Mountain Road. Damages consisted of numerous trees and limbs being blown down, as well as powerlines being downed along Enterprise Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving front across the Mid-Atlantic interacted with a very unstable airmass that was currently in place to produce a severe storm in the Lynchburg area with damaging straight-line winds, resulting in numerous reports of property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe storm produced a swath of wind damage from east of Forest to the Timberlake area, downing many trees, limbs, and powerlines. Some trees and limbs fell on homes. At the Brookeville High School, these severe winds flipped and damaged an entire section of bleachers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the most significant flooding events in years occurred during November 11th and 12th, given both the amounts of rainfall and the footprint where flooding occurred.  In the wake of high pressure passing east off the mid-Atlantic coast, a plume of deep tropical moisture was rapidly carried inland to the central Appalachians ahead of a slow-moving cold front.  Though this plume was of tropical origin, it was not directly related to Tropical Storm Eta, which was off the coast of southern Florida.  Precipitable Water values were in the extremely high range of 1.9 to 2.2 inches with this airmass, around the 99th percentile for mid-November.  In addition, evapotranspiration was significantly lower than during the previous few months given lower daytime heating and the fact that most vegetation had become dormant for the upcoming winter months.  Despite dry conditions for almost two weeks leading up to the event, numerous instances of flooding and flash flooding occurred as 4 to 6 inches of rain fell across portions of the Piedmont on the 11th, followed by an additional 1 to 3 inches on the 12th.  Some of the activity was convective in nature, producing locally 2 to 4 inch per hour rainfall rates.  The rainfall produces numerous instances of flooding and flash flooding, followed shortly by river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ponding of water due to heavy rain was observed across Oakleigh Avenue, causing the road to be impassible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of low pressure and an associated cold front produced two tornadoes across portions of southeast Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the NWS Wakefield office found a path of storm damage consistent with an EF0 tornado. Peak winds were estimated to be 75 mph. The tornado touched down one half mile west of Black Creek Road in Southampton County. The tornado briefly tracked to the east northeast before lifting just northwest of Burdette. The tornado snapped and uprooted several trees along Black Creek Road. In addition, three outbuildings were damaged and a large tree fell on a home causing significant damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front, draped across the foothills and Piedmont and supported aloft by multiple waves of energy entering from the Tennessee Valley, provided the triggers for  scattered slow-moving thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon across the mountains and foothills.  Strong daytime heating provided the instability to allow some storms to intensify to severe levels, producing damaging winds and hail.  In addition, deep tropical moisture in place in the wake of Tropical Storm Isaias allowed some storms to produce heavy rain, with 2-4 inch per hour rainfall rates at times.  Light winds aloft made for slow storm movement, allowing storms to remain over the same locations for extended periods of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff due to a thunderstorm producing heavy rain caused flooding along the Christiansburg Pike, temporarily making the road impassible. A landslide occurred from a roadside hill onto the pike, blocking the road. Radar rainfall estimates indicate that 2 to possibly 3 inches fell in the nearby area where the flooding and the landslide occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to the lower mid-Atlantic region during the period August 12th - 15th, with precipitable water values ranging from 1.6 to 1.9 inches each day.  A frontal boundary stalled near the Interstate 64 corridor provided a trigger for convection, supported aloft by passing upper level disturbances. Strong daytime heating contributed to deep instability, resulting in thunderstorms with high rainfall rates.  Light winds aloft resulted in mean wind speeds generally 10 knots or less, indicating slow cell movement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home weather station recorded nearly 2.1 inches of rain within a 1-hour period.  This was a 25-year average return interval per NOAA Atlas 14 point frequency estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Isaias tracked north just inland of the Middle Atlantic Coast from late Monday night, August 3rd through Tuesday morning, August 4th. Outer bands north and east of the Tropical Storm produced seven tornadoes across portions of eastern and southeast Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado came onshore near the intersection of River Oaks Road and Cypress Isle producing tree damage. The tornado then intensified to 85-90 mph near the intersection of River Oaks Road and Barrets Pointe, where numerous trees were snapped, shingles were blown off roofs, a garage door caved in and a brick gable collapsed. The tornado continued across two fairways of the golf course and entered an area of woods, snapping trees and limbs, before lifting along River Ridge Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Isaias tracked north just inland of the Middle Atlantic Coast from late Monday night, August 3rd through Tuesday morning, August 4th. Outer bands north and east of the Tropical Storm produced seven tornadoes across portions of eastern and southeast Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the NWS Wakefield office found a path of storm damage consistent with an EF1 tornado. The tornado likely developed as a waterspout over the York River, then moved onshore at Middle Peninsula State Park. The tornado then quickly tracked north northwest, crossing over Rosewell Plantation, Gum Point Lane and Taliaferro Lane. Numerous trees were snapped and uprooted in this area consistent with EF1 damage. Some roof materials and siding were also removed from homes in this area. The tornado then briefly moved over the York River again before crossing over Hermitage Lane and Primrose Path where additional snapped trees were observed. The tornado continued to move north northwest before lifting near Cappahosic Road.

The tornado snapped or uprooted numerous trees along its path and also one carport was rolled and destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front over the Appalachians interacted with numerous westward propagating boundaries formed from morning convection along the East Coast. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe storm producing damaging winds blew two trees down on the western part of Christiansburg, VA. One fell on Harman Circle drive. The second fell near the intersection of Riner Rd. and Smith Creek Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Isaias tracked north just inland of the Middle Atlantic Coast from late Monday night, August 3rd through Tuesday morning, August 4th. Winds associated with the tropical storm caused moderate (tidal) coastal flooding across portions of the Virginia Eastern Shore adjacent to the Chesapeake Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southeast winds associated with Tropical Storm Isaias allowed some water to pile up in the mid to upper Chesapeake Bay early Tuesday morning. Then, when winds shifted to more of a west or northwest direction on the southern flank of Isaias during the mid to late morning on Tuesday, water quickly got pushed into areas on the bay side of the Lower Eastern Shore (and water levels in these locations rose very rapidly). This caused moderate (tidal) coastal flooding over portions of Accomack county. Saxis reached 5.63 feet MLLW late Tuesday morning on August 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to the lower mid-Atlantic region during the period August 12th - 15th, with precipitable water values ranging from 1.6 to 1.9 inches each day.  A frontal boundary stalled near the Interstate 64 corridor provided a trigger for convection, supported aloft by passing upper level disturbances. Strong daytime heating contributed to deep instability, resulting in thunderstorms with high rainfall rates.  Light winds aloft resulted in mean wind speeds generally 10 knots or less, indicating slow cell movement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A partially blocked culvert caused Dalton Creek to rise out of its banks near the Fries School, flooding the parking lot up to the building.  Radar rainfall estimates of about 1.5 inches fell within the 0.54 mi^2 (USGS Stream Stats) Dalton Creek watershed, with a personal mesonet station in the town of Fries recorded 1.48 inches rainfall in a 30-minute period, roughly a 10-year rainfall event per NOAA Atlas-14 point frequency estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to the lower mid-Atlantic region during the period August 12th - 15th, with precipitable water values ranging from 1.6 to 1.9 inches each day. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms produced 1.5 to 2 inches of rain within about an hour across the town of Tazewell. FLASH data indicates this amount to be 1.5 to 1.7 times greater than 1-hour flash flood guidance for this area. CREST unit streamflow for this event was 550 cfs/mi^2, while the 1-hour average return interval was around a 25-year event. Runoff from this rainfall caused flooding of several roads in the vicinity of Dogwood Road and Steeles Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the most significant flooding events in years occurred during November 11th and 12th, given both the amounts of rainfall and the footprint where flooding occurred.  In the wake of high pressure passing east off the mid-Atlantic coast, a plume of deep tropical moisture was rapidly carried inland to the central Appalachians ahead of a slow-moving cold front.  Though this plume was of tropical origin, it was not directly related to Tropical Storm Eta, which was off the coast of southern Florida.  Precipitable Water values were in the extremely high range of 1.9 to 2.2 inches with this airmass, around the 99th percentile for mid-November.  In addition, evapotranspiration was significantly lower than during the previous few months given lower daytime heating and the fact that most vegetation had become dormant for the upcoming winter months.  Despite dry conditions for almost two weeks leading up to the event, numerous instances of flooding and flash flooding occurred as 4 to 6 inches of rain fell across portions of the Piedmont on the 11th, followed by an additional 1 to 3 inches on the 12th.  Some of the activity was convective in nature, producing locally 2 to 4 inch per hour rainfall rates.  The rainfall produces numerous instances of flooding and flash flooding, followed shortly by river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain was observed ponding across Oakley Avenue, briefly closing the road until the water receded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cooperative Weather Observer at Galax, VA (GAAV2) observed a 72-hour (ending 12Z on November 12th) precipitation amount of 5.76 inches.  This was the highest 72-hour total for this station during the month of November.  The 48-hour total (ending 12Z on November 12th) of 5.63 inches was also the highest 48-hour total for November and 7th highest all-time.  The 24-hour total of 3.81 inches measured at 12Z on the 12th is the highest 24-hour measurement for November.  These amounts are 10- to 25-year ARI events at the 48- and 72-hour durations, and 5- to 10-year events at the 24-hour duration per NOAA Atlas 14 point precipitation estimates.  Records date back to May 1907.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Isaias tracked north just inland of the Middle Atlantic Coast from late Monday night, August 3rd through Tuesday morning, August 4th. Outer bands north and east of the Tropical Storm produced seven tornadoes across portions of eastern and southeast Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the NWS Wakefield office found a path of storm damage consistent with an EF1 tornado. The tornado moved onshore from the Chesapeake Bay in the town of Fleeton, crossing Fleeton Road where several houses experienced roof and siding damage. In addition, trees were snapped and uprooted. The tornado then crossed over the Wicomico River and then moved back onshore near Timbs Road where trees were uprooted and snapped, and a few houses experienced roof and siding damage. The tornado then moved northward to near Buzzards Point Road, destroying a canopy, before lifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked into the Ohio Valley before transferring its energy to a coastal low along the Mid-Atlantic Coast. The coastal low intensified as it moved to the northeast. At the same time, high pressure was located to the north. Therefore, significant wintry precipitation fell across the area in the form of snow, sleet, and freezing rain. Snowfall amounts averaged around 6 to 12 inches near and west of the Blue Ridge Mountains with 2 to 4 inches west of Interstate 95. Freezing rain also occurred with around a quarter to one-half inch of ice near the Blue Ridge Mountains in northern and central Virginia and ice amounts around a tenth of an inch just west of Interstate 95.

The center of Tropical Storm Isaias tracked north just inland of the Middle Atlantic Coast from late Monday night, August 3rd through Tuesday morning, August 4th. Outer bands north and east of the Tropical Storm produced seven tornadoes across portions of eastern and southeast Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the NWS Wakefield office found a path of storm damage consistent with an EF2 tornado. The tornado touched down near Windmill Point Lane then rapidly intensified as it crossed over Antipoison Creek. Numerous houses in the Antipoison Neck area along Tammy Drive, Clark Point Drive, Deep Water Drive, Wayne Circle, Chesapeake Trail, and others experienced significant roof loss and structural damage consistent with EF2 intensity. One house had the upper portions shifted off the main structure. Similar roof and structural damage continued as the tornado moved north into areas near Ocran Road, Squires Road and Bald Eagle Road in the Poplar Neck area. This is also where the tornado was at its widest point of 500 yards. The tornado then continued northward to Fleets Bay Road where additional roof loss consistent with EF2 intensity occurred. After this point, the tornado likely weakened to an EF1 as it crossed Boys Camp Road, Waverly Avenue and East Church Street in Kilmarnock. Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped in this area, with some siding and roof damage. The tornado then continued north northwest across Carlson Road and Mount Olive Road where more downed trees were observed, along with some roof and siding damage to houses. The tornado then tracked from Lancaster county into Northumberland county.

The center of Tropical Storm Isaias tracked north just inland of the Middle Atlantic Coast from late Monday night, August 3rd through Tuesday morning, August 4th. Outer bands north and east of the Tropical Storm produced seven tornadoes across portions of eastern and southeast Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the NWS Wakefield office found a path of storm damage consistent with an EF2 tornado. The damage began near the Southampton Power Station off General Thomas Highway and ended 4 miles north of Sebrell near Farmers Bridge Road. The tornado first touched down in a wooded area and caused numerous trees to be snapped about 6 miles southeast of Courtland. The tornado then moved northeast and into Courtland, where it caused damage to numerous homes and businesses along Highway 58, including lifting the second story roof off a hotel building. Several vehicles were also overturned. The tornado then continued to travel northeast where more trees were snapped or uprooted. The tornado finally lifted just north of Sebrell near Farmers Bridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Zeta passed quickly across the lower mid-Atlantic region on the morning of the 29th, but still brought 2-3 inches of rainfall to the foothills of the Blue Ridge and the Piedmont region.  Much of this rain fell within a 1-2 hour period, resulting in rapid flooding of several small streams.  Residual moisture in the wake of Zeta allowed for redevelopment of bands of slow-moving storms producing additional heavy rain, which also resulted in localized flooding across the Piedmont of Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms formed along a boundary left in the wake of Tropical Storm Zeta, producing 2.5-4 inches of rain in a three to four hour period.  Rainfall rates were observed to be one to two inches per hour.  The Campbell County 911 Center reported that at least seven roads within the central part of the county were flooded and impassable.  This included Yellow Branch rising out of its banks near the intersection of Morman Mill Road and Patterson Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Zeta passed quickly across the lower mid-Atlantic region on the morning of the 29th, but still brought 2-3 inches of rainfall to the foothills of the Blue Ridge and the Piedmont region. Much of this rain fell within a 1-2 hour period, resulting in rapid flooding of several small streams. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple weather stations in the southeastern portion of Franklin County recorded between two to locally 3.5 inches of rainfall inside a three hour period during the passage of Tropical Storm Zeta.  Rainfall rates of one to two inches per hour were observed in some locations.  This rain caused multiple streams to flood out of their banks onto nearby roadways in the Snow Creek area, making them impassible until the water receded.  This includes Muddy Fork Road flooded by Reed Creek and Sutton Hollow Road closed due to flooding of Crawford Creek and Snow Creek. Fawndale Road and portions of the Virgil Goode Highway were also reported flooded and impassible for a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad closed upper low drifted across South Carolina and northwest back into southwest Virginia and West Virginia bringing persistent rainfall that eventually led to flooding. There were repeated rounds of heavy rainfall with widespread amounts of 2 to 5 inches, with isolated totals over 10 inches in a 3-day period in parts of Halifax County, VA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station at Keysville 2 S (KYSV2) measured 4.17 inches of rain in the 24-hour period ending 700 AM EDT which was the highest 1-day total  on record for any June day. The previous June record was 3.75 inches June 2, 1997. It was also the 6th highest for a calendar day. Precipitation data at this site dates back to 1979 with some breaks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad closed upper low drifted across South Carolina and northwest back into southwest Virginia and West Virginia bringing persistent rainfall that eventually led to flooding. There were repeated rounds of heavy rainfall with widespread amounts of 2 to 5 inches, with isolated totals over 10 inches in a 3-day period in parts of Halifax County, VA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station at South Boston (SOBV2) had a 24-hour rain total of 5.55 inches ending at 700 AM on the 18th, the 2nd highest on record in the month of June, after the 6.98 inches on June 23rd, 1995. It was also the 4th highest for any calendar day. The station also had a 2-day rain total of 8.80 on and a 3-day total of 10.54 inches ending 700 AM on the 18th. This was the highest 2-day total for June. beating June 24, 1995 with 8.02 inches. It was also 2nd highest amount for any calendar 2-day period. The still-existing 2-day record is 8.83 inches September 6-7, 1996 during the passage of remains from Hurricane Fran. Data extends back to 1981 at this site. The 2-day, 8.80 inch amount is in the 90 percent confidence interval (7.90-9.57 inches) for a 100-year rain event (.01 annual chance of occurrence) per NOAA Atlas 14. The 3-day total was also a new record for the site, exceeding the 9.87 inches that also occurred during Fran in September, 1996. The 3-day, 10.54 inch amount is in the 90 percent confidence interval (9.29-11.4 inches) for a 200-year event (.005 annual chance of occurrence) per NOAA Atlas 14. Data extends back to 1981 at this station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad closed upper low drifted across South Carolina and northwest back into southwest Virginia and West Virginia bringing persistent rainfall that eventually led to flooding. There were repeated rounds of heavy rainfall with widespread amounts of 2 to 5 inches, with isolated totals over 10 inches in a 3-day period in parts of Halifax County, VA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS Cooperative station at Rocky Mount, VA (RYMV2) set a date rainfall record for June 18th of 2.65 inches for the 24-hour period ending 700 AM EDT on the 18th. The previous date record was 1.63 inches set in 1940. Nearly unbroken precipitation records exist at this station back to 1894, one of the longest running sites in the WFO Blacksburg area of responsibility.

A broad closed upper low drifted across South Carolina and northwest back into southwest Virginia and West Virginia bringing persistent rainfall that eventually led to flooding. There were repeated rounds of heavy rainfall with widespread amounts of 2 to 5 inches, with isolated totals over 10 inches in a 3-day period in parts of Halifax County, VA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS Cooperative station at Copper Hill (COHV2) had its 2nd wettest June day on record, and 4th wettest of any calendar day, as 6.65 inches of rain fell in the 24-hours ending 700 AM EDT on the  18th. The existing June (and all-time) record is 8.21 inches on June 21, 1972 during the remnants of Hurricane Agnes. Per NOAA Atlas 14, for a 24-hour period, this rainfall total is in the 90 percent confidence interval (6.37 to 7.72 inches) for a 50-year recurrence (.02 annual exceedance probability). Complete data extends back to 1940 at this site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Zeta passed quickly across the lower mid-Atlantic region on the morning of the 29th, but still brought 2-3 inches of rainfall to the foothills of the Blue Ridge and the Piedmont region.  Much of this rain fell within a 1-2 hour period, resulting in rapid flooding of several small streams.  Residual moisture in the wake of Zeta allowed for redevelopment of bands of slow-moving storms producing additional heavy rain, which also resulted in localized flooding across the Piedmont of Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two roads flooded in the northern part of Pittsylvania county where up to three inches of rain fell from Tropical Cyclone Zeta. The roads were closed until the water receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked into the Ohio Valley before transferring its energy to a coastal low along the Mid-Atlantic Coast. The coastal low intensified as it moved to the northeast. At the same time, high pressure was located to the north. Therefore, significant wintry precipitation fell across the area in the form of snow, sleet, and freezing rain. Snowfall amounts averaged around 6 to 12 inches near and west of the Blue Ridge Mountains with 2 to 4 inches west of Interstate 95. Freezing rain also occurred with around a quarter to one-half inch of ice near the Blue Ridge Mountains in northern and central Virginia and ice amounts around a tenth of an inch just west of Interstate 95.
A cold northwest wind behind a cold front caused wind chills to drop to between 10 and 20 degrees below zero along and west of the Allegheny Front.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roanoke River at Brookneal (BROV2) crested above moderate flood stage at 27.74 feet, which is about 35,100 cfs.  This is between a two and five year average return interval per USGS analysis for this gauge.  Typical impacts at this stage are that the nearby low-lying fields and woods are flooded, though the Brookneal Fish Hatchery pond furthest downstream may begin to experience some flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds associated with Tropical Storm Zeta caused damage and power outages in southwestern Virginia, concentrated close to border with North Carolina. Wind gusts reached 30-40 knots during the peak of the storm. Numerous trees were blown down by Tropical Storm Zeta, with many falling on homes, power lines, and blocking roadways. A thunderstorm closely following Zeta contributed to some of the wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down across Charlotte County, blocking several roadways. Several power lines were also brought down around the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Zeta passed quickly across the lower mid-Atlantic region on the morning of the 29th, but still brought 2-3 inches of rainfall to the foothills of the Blue Ridge and the Piedmont region.  Much of this rain fell within a 1-2 hour period, resulting in rapid flooding of several small streams.  Residual moisture in the wake of Zeta allowed for redevelopment of bands of slow-moving storms producing additional heavy rain, which also resulted in localized flooding across the Piedmont of Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cooperative Weather Observer at Buckingham (BUKV2) recorded a 24-hour rainfall measurement ending at 7am EDT on October 30th of 3.54 inches. This was the fourth highest 1-day rainfall measurement during the month of October for this station since it was opened in May 1894.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked into the Ohio Valley before transferring its energy to a coastal low along the Mid-Atlantic Coast. The coastal low intensified as it moved to the northeast. At the same time, high pressure was located to the north. Therefore, significant wintry precipitation fell across the area in the form of snow, sleet, and freezing rain. Snowfall amounts averaged around 6 to 12 inches near and west of the Blue Ridge Mountains with 2 to 4 inches west of Interstate 95. Freezing rain also occurred with around a quarter to one-half inch of ice near the Blue Ridge Mountains in northern and central Virginia and ice amounts around a tenth of an inch just west of Interstate 95.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation totaled up to a half inch at Opal.

Winds associated with Tropical Storm Zeta caused damage and power outages in southwestern Virginia, concentrated close to border with North Carolina. Wind gusts reached 30-40 knots during the peak of the storm. Numerous trees were blown down by Tropical Storm Zeta, with many falling on homes, power lines, and blocking roadways. A thunderstorm closely following Zeta contributed to some of the wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two dozen trees were blown down across Patrick County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple weather stations in the vicinity of the Blue Ridge in Carroll County indicate that between 1.5 and 2.5 inches of rain fell inside a two-hour period during the passage of Tropical Storm Zeta at rates of one to two inches per hour in spots. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple personal weather stations in the upper Falling River basin indicated three to four inches of rain fell during the late evening hours of the 29th into the early morning hours of the 30th.  Runoff from this rain collected in the Falling River, with the gauge at the Falling River near Naruna (FLRV2) rising to 20.39 feet (10600 cfs).  This is between a 5- and 10-year average return interval for this river gauge per USGS stream statistics.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple data sources across all of Patrick County, including trained spotters and personal automated weather stations, indicate that between two and locally three inches of rain fell during a two-hour period during the passage of Tropical Storm Zeta from roughly 9 am to 11 am EDT.  Rainfall rates were observed at one to two inches per hour in spots.  This rain caused multiple streams to flood out of their banks onto nearby roadways, some up to 12 inches deep in the vicinity of Buffalo Creek, making them impassible until the water receded.  This included Egg Farm Road where it intersected Grassy Branch Creek, as water was estimated to be at least eight inches deep over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper-level trough interacted with a modestly unstable environment over the Middle Atlantic to produce several supercell thunderstorms which transitioned into small bowing line segments across far eastern West Virginia, central Maryland and northern Virginia. The storms produce substantial and relatively widespread wind damage and some hail along their path, though the storms themselves were isolated to widely scattered in nature.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 55-knot (63 MPH) wind gust was measured by the military ASOS at Quantico. Trees were blown down on US-1 Jefferson Davis Highway adjacent to the base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper-level trough interacted with a modestly unstable environment over the Middle Atlantic to produce several supercell thunderstorms which transitioned into small bowing line segments across far eastern West Virginia, central Maryland and northern Virginia. The storms produce substantial and relatively widespread wind damage and some hail along their path, though the storms themselves were isolated to widely scattered in nature.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported near Jefferson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front swept across southwest Virginia Christmas Eve. Rain changed to a period of heavy snow during the evening with a period of snowfall rates of near two inches an hour. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 7 inches in Dickenson and Buchanan Counties, leading to treacherous road conditions and a few areas of significant car backups on the evening of December 24th. Snow showers remained in the air all of Christmas Day bringing some additional accumulations.  This system produced our first white Christmas in several years for many locations.

Low pressure tracked northeast along the east coast of the U.S. Moisture from this system fell as a wintry mix across parts of southwest Virginia. Snow, freezing rain, and sleet impacted the region with the result being a slushy and slick mix of the above on roads and other surfaces. Snowfall amounts were mostly in the one to two inch range with some isolated locations seeing two to three inches. Ice accretion ranged mainly from one-tenth to one-quarter inch, but isolated pockets of three-tenths to six-tenths of an inch of ice also occurred. Accumulation of sleet was generally less than one inch but with isolated two inch amounts. Key Impacts: Slick and slushy roads and sidewalks, downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light amounts of snow fell across the county. Ice accretion totaled one-quarter inch one mile northeast of Pulaski. At a location three miles east-northeast of Dublin four-tenths of an inch of sleet fell. Key Impacts: Slick and slushy roads and sidewalks.

Sleet feel to a depth of four-tenths of an inch at both three miles east-northeast of Troutdale and four miles east of Whitetop. Key Impacts: Slick and slushy roads and sidewalks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought heavy snow to parts of Southwest Virginia. The storm began with rain during the early morning hours of 12/24. In the afternoon, cold air rapidly swept into the area, changing rain to snow. Moderate to heavy snow fell for about 3-4 hours that evening, resulting in several inches of accumulation. Most of Southwest Virginia reported between 2 and 6 inches of snow. Scattered to numerous snow showers continued into Christmas Day, with only light additional accumulations.

A strong cold front swept across the region during the evening hours of Christmas Eve. Along and in advance of this cold front, very gusty winds occurred. Some were strong enough to down trees and limbs in parts of Roanoke and Smyth Counties. While rain preceded the cold front, sub-freezing air rapidly entered the area after the passage of the cold front, dropping the temperatures some five to eight degrees per hour. This quickly turned the rain to snow, and resulted in flash freeze of surface moisture to slippery ice. The snow quickly accumulated during the night, with parts of southwest Virginia receiving four to six inches of snow. Key impacts: slippery roads and sidewalks, falling trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall across the county ranged from 4.0 inches at Mouth of Wilson, to 5.0 inches five miles northwest of Birdie Creek, to 6.0 inches at Baywood. Key impacts: slippery roads and sidewalks.
